members.summary: Bounding-box handling policy for `Compose` (Albumentations-style).
members.extended:
When a `Compose` is constructed with `bbox_params=...`,
it records every `lucid.utils.transforms.BoundingBoxes`'s
*pre-pipeline* area, runs the pipeline, then calls
`lucid.utils.transforms._datatypes.filter_boxes` on the
result with these thresholds.  Out-of-frame / heavily-occluded
boxes — and their labels — are dropped in one place rather than
re-implemented inside every geometric transform.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description:
Albumentations format name.  Informational only; each
`lucid.utils.transforms.BoundingBoxes` already carries
its own `format` and conversion is handled per-instance.

parameters.name: min_area
parameters.description:
Drop post-transform boxes whose absolute pixel area is below
this (units: pixels² in the post-transform canvas).

parameters.name: min_visibility
parameters.description:
Drop boxes whose visible fraction (area / original area) falls
below this.  The ratio is computed against the
`lucid.utils.transforms._datatypes.box_areas` snapshot
recorded *before* the pipeline ran.

parameters.name: label_fields
parameters.description:
Reserved for API parity with Albumentations; in Lucid, labels
already travel on `BoundingBoxes.labels` and are trimmed
in lock-step.  No-op here.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.Compose(
...     [T.Crop(0, 0, 50, 50)],
...     bbox_params=T.BboxParams(min_area=10.0, min_visibility=0.5),
... )

parameters.name: bbox_params
parameters.description:
When given, bounding boxes are filtered after the pipeline by
`min_area` / `min_visibility` (degenerate boxes that left the
frame are dropped, along with their labels).

examples:
>>> from lucid.utils.transforms import Compose, Resize, CenterCrop, Normalize
>>> tf = Compose([

...     CenterCrop(224, 224),

...               max_pixel_value=1.0),
... ])
>>> y = tf(image)

members.summary: Wraps `(N, 4)` bounding boxes with a format + canvas size.
members.extended:
A typed target consumed by every `GeometricTransform` —
flips / crops / resizes update both the coordinates and the
reported `canvas_size` consistently.  Optional `labels` ride
along the same row dimension so `filter_boxes` can drop
out-of-frame boxes + their labels in one step.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description:
`(N, 4)` coordinates interpreted under `format`.  For the
absolute formats (`xyxy` / `xywh` / `cxcywh`) units are
pixels; for the normalized formats (`yolo` / `albumentations`)
units are fractions of `canvas_size`.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description:
"pascal_voc", "coco"}
Coordinate convention.  `"pascal_voc"` is an alias for
`"xyxy"`; `"coco"` for `"xywh"`.  See
`normalize_box_format`.

parameters.name: canvas_size
parameters.description:
`(H, W)` of the image the boxes index into.  Geometric
transforms (crop / resize / pad / rotate) re-emit a new
`BoundingBoxes` with the matching canvas.

parameters.name: labels
parameters.description:
`(N,)` class indices (or any per-box scalar — score, track
id, ...).  Carried through every geometric transform; trimmed
in lock-step with rows by `filter_boxes`.  Default
`None` (no per-box labels).

raises.description:
If `format` (after alias normalisation) is not one of the
recognised names.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> boxes = T.BoundingBoxes(
...     lucid.tensor([[10.0, 10.0, 40.0, 40.0]]),
...     "xyxy",
...     canvas_size=(100, 100),
...     labels=lucid.tensor([1.0]),
... )
>>> out = T.HorizontalFlip(p=1.0)({"image": T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 100, 100)),
...                                "boxes": boxes})
>>> out["boxes"].labels.numpy().tolist()
[1.0]
notes:
Format conventions (each row's 4 values):

* `xyxy` — `(x_min, y_min, x_max, y_max)`
* `xywh` — `(x_min, y_min, width, height)`
* `cxcywh` — `(cx, cy, width, height)`
* `yolo` — `(cx, cy, w, h)`, all in `[0, 1]`
* `albumentations` — `(x_min, y_min, x_max, y_max)`, all in `[0, 1]`

on `isinstance` and route a pixel image differently from a

configured interpolation mode.

members.summary: Wraps a segmentation / label mask, resampled with nearest mode.
members.extended:
Companion target for semantic / instance segmentation pipelines.
Geometric transforms (flip / crop / resize / rotate) move the mask
in lock-step with its image but always resample with *nearest*
interpolation so class indices survive unblended; photometric
transforms (colour-jitter, blur, ...) leave the mask untouched.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description:
Label / probability map with shape `(H, W)`, `(C, H, W)`,
or `(B, C, H, W)`.  Integer dtypes are recommended for class
indices to preserve nearest-neighbour resampling exactly.

members.summary: Crop a centred `height` x `width` window (Albumentations `CenterCrop`).
members.extended:
Computes a deterministic crop centred on the input — top-left at
`((H - height) // 2, (W - width) // 2)` — and applies the same
offsets to mask / boxes / keypoints so every target stays aligned.

parameters.name: p

ends up at most as large as `max_size`.  Image uses
`interpolation`, masks use nearest.

parameters.description:
Image tensor used only to fix the parameter dtype / dispatch

Carries `ksize` — the odd box-kernel side length sampled
uniformly from `[3, blur_limit]`.

notes:
Even draws are forced to the next odd integer so the kernel

members.summary: Box (mean) blur with a random odd kernel size (Albumentations `Blur`).
members.extended:
Samples an odd kernel size `k` uniformly from `[3, blur_limit]`
and convolves the image with a normalised `k x k` all-ones kernel
via `functional.depthwise_conv2d`.

parameters.name: blur_limit
parameters.description:
Inclusive upper bound on the sampled kernel size; values are
rounded up to the next odd integer to keep the kernel centred.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

members.name: Defocus
members.signature: Defocus(radius: tuple[int, int] = (3, 10), p: float = 0.5)

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `radius` — the disk-kernel radius in pixels
drawn uniformly from the constructor's `radius` range.

members.summary: Disk-kernel (out-of-focus) blur (Albumentations `Defocus`).
members.extended:
Samples an integer radius `r` from `radius` (inclusive) and
convolves with a normalised disk of radius `r` — pixels inside
the disk get weight `1 / area`, those outside get zero.
Approximates the bokeh circle of an out-of-focus lens better than
Gaussian blur.

parameters.name: radius
parameters.description:
Inclusive sampling range for the disk radius in pixels.
Larger radii produce more aggressive defocus.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

Carries `scale` — the downscale factor sampled uniformly

members.summary: Downscale then upscale to lose high-frequency detail (Albumentations `Downscale`).
members.extended:
Samples a scale factor uniformly from `[scale_min, scale_max]`,
resizes the image to that fraction of its original `(H, W)` via
nearest-neighbour, then resizes back to the original size — a
cheap way to simulate sensor / JPEG block-level degradation.

parameters.name: scale_min
parameters.description: Lower bound of the downscale factor.

parameters.name: scale_max
parameters.description:
Upper bound of the downscale factor (set equal to
`scale_min` for a deterministic factor).

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

methods.name: make_params

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `ksize` (odd kernel side from `blur_limit`) and
`sigma` (Gaussian standard deviation).

notes:
A non-positive sampled `sigma` falls back to OpenCV's
kernel-derived default `0.3 * ((k - 1) * 0.5 - 1.0) + 0.8`
so the Gaussian matches cv2's auto-sigma policy.

members.summary: Gaussian blur with random kernel size and sigma (Albumentations `GaussianBlur`).
members.extended:
Samples an odd kernel size from `blur_limit` and a sigma from
`sigma_limit`; when sigma is left at zero the OpenCV default
`0.3 * ((k - 1) * 0.5 - 1.0) + 0.8` is used so the kernel matches
cv2's auto-sigma policy.

parameters.name: blur_limit
parameters.description:
Inclusive range for the kernel size; both ends are rounded up
to the next odd integer.

parameters.name: sigma_limit
parameters.description:
Range for the Gaussian sigma.  A scalar `v` expands to
`(0.0, v)`; `0` triggers the OpenCV auto-sigma formula.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

Image tensor; not inspected, only carried through for the

Carries `ksize` — the odd window side length sampled
uniformly from `[3, blur_limit]`.

notes:
Even draws are forced to the next odd integer so the median

members.summary: Median filter with a random odd kernel (Albumentations `MedianBlur`).
members.extended:
Samples an odd kernel size `k` uniformly from `[3, blur_limit]`,
builds the `k * k` shifted-neighbour stack via `lucid.roll`,
sorts along the stack axis, and returns the median element — an
impulse-noise denoiser that preserves edges better than `Blur`.

parameters.name: blur_limit
parameters.description:
Inclusive upper bound on the sampled kernel size; rounded up
to the next odd integer so the window is centred.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

members.name: MotionBlur
members.signature: MotionBlur(blur_limit: int = 7, p: float = 0.5)

methods.name: make_params

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `ksize` (odd kernel side, `[3, blur_limit]`)
and `angle` in degrees sampled uniformly from
`[0, 180)`.

notes:
Even kernel-size draws are forced to the next odd integer.
Angles outside `[0, 180)` are unnecessary since the streak
kernel is symmetric under 180° rotation.

members.summary: Directional (linear streak) motion blur (Albumentations `MotionBlur`).
members.extended:
Samples an odd kernel size and a streak angle in `[0, 180)°`, draws
a 1-pixel-wide line through the kernel centre at that angle, then
convolves the normalised kernel with the image — approximates the
look of a camera panning while the shutter is open.

parameters.name: blur_limit
parameters.description:
Inclusive upper bound on the kernel size; rounded up to the
next odd integer.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(multiplier: tuple[float, float] = (0.9, 1.1), elementwise: bool = False, p: float = 0.5)

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries the constructor's `multiplier` bounds verbatim
(`lo`, `hi`).  The actual scalar / per-pixel multiplier
draw is deferred to the apply step so `elementwise` can
switch between the two regimes without re-running the
sampling head.

members.summary: Multiply pixel values by random noise (Albumentations `MultiplicativeNoise`).
members.extended:
With `elementwise=False` (default) a single scalar is sampled
per call and the whole image is scaled by it; with
`elementwise=True` an independent multiplier is sampled per
pixel.  The output is always clipped back to `[0, 1]`.

parameters.name: multiplier
parameters.description: Inclusive range of the per-pixel (or per-image) multiplier.

parameters.name: elementwise
parameters.description:
If `True`, sample one multiplier per pixel rather than one
per image.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

members.name: ZoomBlur
members.signature: ZoomBlur(max_factor: float = 1.31, step_factor: tuple[float, float] = (0.01, 0.03), p: float = 0.5)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(max_factor: float = 1.31, step_factor: tuple[float, float] = (0.01, 0.03), p: float = 0.5)

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `factor` — the final zoom factor drawn uniformly
from `[1.0, max_factor]`.  The apply step interpolates
5 intermediate zoom levels between `1.0` and `factor`.

members.summary: Radial zoom blur via averaged centre-zoomed copies (Albumentations `ZoomBlur`).
members.extended:
Samples a final zoom factor uniformly from `[1.0, max_factor]`,
builds 5 centre-crops at progressively-increasing zoom levels
(each resized back to the input `(H, W)` via bilinear), and
averages them with the original image — simulates a fast forward
dolly into the scene.

parameters.name: max_factor
parameters.description: Upper bound on the final zoom factor (must be `>= 1.0`).

parameters.name: step_factor
parameters.description:

members.name: OneOf
members.signature: OneOf(transforms: list[TransformLike], p: float = 0.5)
members.summary: Apply exactly one child, sampled by child-`p` weight (Albumentations `OneOf`).
members.extended:
On each call (gated by the container's `p`), draws a single child
via a categorical distribution whose weights are the children's
own `p` values normalised to sum to 1.  The selected child is
then forced to run with its own probability gate bypassed — its
`p` acts purely as a *selection weight*, not a per-call
skip-probability.  Equal weights ⇒ uniform sampling.

parameters.name: transforms
parameters.description:
Candidate children.  Each child's `p` is used as its
unnormalised selection weight; raw callables get weight 1.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description:
Container-level gate — with probability `1 - p` the entire
block is skipped and the input passes through unchanged.

examples:
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.OneOf([
...     T.HorizontalFlip(p=1.0),
...     T.VerticalFlip(p=1.0),
... ], p=1.0)

members.name: OneOrOther
members.signature: OneOrOther(first: TransformLike, second: TransformLike, p: float = 0.5)
members.summary: Apply `first` with probability `p` else `second` (Albumentations `OneOrOther`).
members.extended:
A two-way categorical: every call invokes either `first` or
`second`, never both, never neither.  The selected child is
forced to run with its own probability gate bypassed — `p` here
is the *switch* between the two branches, not a skip-probability,
so the container has effective probability 1.

parameters.name: first
parameters.description: Child invoked when the per-call uniform sample is below `p`.

parameters.name: second
parameters.description: Child invoked otherwise.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Switch probability; `0.5` gives the two branches equal odds.

examples:
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.OneOrOther(
...     first=T.HorizontalFlip(p=1.0),
...     second=T.VerticalFlip(p=1.0),
...     p=0.7,
... )

methods.name: replay
methods.signature: replay(saved: list[tuple[TransformLike, object, bool]], inputs: object)
methods.summary: Re-apply a previously recorded `replay_data` to a new sample.
methods.extended:
Walks the saved `(transform, params, applied)` log in order
and dispatches each `Transform` with its *original*
sampled params (no fresh sampling).  Raw callables are
re-invoked without parameters.  Records marked `applied=False`
are skipped, preserving the original gate decisions.

parameters.name: saved
parameters.description:
Trace produced by an earlier `__call__`; usually
`other_compose.replay_data`.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Sample to feed through the replayed pipeline.

returns.description: The replayed output, structurally matching `inputs`.

members.summary: Compose that records applied params for deterministic replay.
members.extended:
After a call, `replay_data` holds `(transform, params, applied)`
per child; `replay` re-applies the *same* params to a new
sample (e.g. to apply an identical augmentation to a paired input).

members.summary: Apply all children in order, gated by `p` (Albumentations `Sequential`).
members.extended:
The container's `p` controls whether the whole block runs at
all on a given call — when the gate fails, every child is
skipped and the input passes through unchanged.  When the gate
passes, each child is invoked left-to-right with its *own*
probability gate honoured (unlike `OneOf` / `SomeOf`
which bypass the child gate).

Block-level probability — when below 1, the chain is a
no-op on `1 - p` of calls.

members.name: BBoxSafeRandomCrop
members.signature: BBoxSafeRandomCrop(erosion_rate: float = 0.0, p: float = 1.0)
members.summary: Random crop guaranteed to contain every bounding box (Albumentations `BBoxSafeRandomCrop`).
members.extended:
Computes the tightest axis-aligned window enclosing every box in
the sample, then jitters the four edges outward into the
surrounding image — picking a uniformly random crop within that
enlarged region.  The resulting crop always fully contains every
input box, so detection / instance-segmentation labels never need
to be filtered.  If the sample carries no boxes the transform
degenerates into a no-op identity crop covering the full image.

parameters.name: erosion_rate
parameters.description:
Fraction by which the enclosing box-union is *shrunk* before
the jittered window is drawn.  `0` keeps every box fully
inside; positive values let the window cut into boxes by up
to `erosion_rate` of their extent (Albumentations parity).

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

examples:
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.BBoxSafeRandomCrop(p=1.0)

members.name: CLAHE
members.signature: CLAHE(clip_limit: float = 4.0, tile_grid_size: tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), p: float = 0.5)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(clip_limit: float = 4.0, tile_grid_size: tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), p: float = 0.5)

members.summary: Contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (Albumentations `CLAHE`).
members.extended:
Thin wrapper around `functional.clahe`: per-tile
clipped-histogram LUT + 4-neighbour bilinear interpolation between
tile centres.  Single-channel images are equalized directly; RGB
is routed through the HSV value channel so hue and saturation are
preserved (mirrors Albumentations' "luminance-only" behaviour).

parameters.name: clip_limit
parameters.description:
Contrast-clip threshold; per-tile histogram cap is
`clip_limit * tile_area / 256`.  Higher values allow more
contrast amplification at the cost of noise.

parameters.name: tile_grid_size
parameters.description:
Number of tiles `(rows, cols)` the image is divided into.
Larger grids give more local adaptivity at a per-tile cost.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.CLAHE(clip_limit=4.0, tile_grid_size=(8, 8), p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 64, 64))).data
>>> tuple(out.shape)
(3, 64, 64)
notes:
Approximate cv2 / Albumentations parity (colour-space pivot
differs: HSV value vs LAB L).  Currently ~30× slower than Albu
on a 224² RGB input — see `functional.clahe` notes for the
bottleneck description.

members.summary: AlexNet-style PCA colour augmentation (Krizhevsky 2012, Albumentations `FancyPCA`).
members.extended:
Computes the per-image RGB covariance matrix, takes its
eigendecomposition, and adds a random linear combination of the
eigenvectors weighted by their eigenvalues to every pixel.  The
weights are i.i.d. samples from `alpha * U(-1, 1)` per channel —
same recipe used in the original AlexNet training pipeline.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Standard deviation scale on the eigenvalue-weighted perturbation;
larger values give more colour jitter.

parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

raises.description: If the input image does not have exactly 3 channels.

>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.FancyPCA(alpha=0.1, p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 32, 32))).data
>>> tuple(out.shape)
(3, 32, 32)

members.name: Posterize
members.signature: Posterize(num_bits: int = 4, mode: str = 'uint8_mask', p: float = 0.5)

methods.name: make_params
methods.summary: Return the no-op parameter sentinel for `Posterize`.

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected.

returns.description:
The shared `NO_PARAMS` sentinel — Posterize is
deterministic given `num_bits` / `mode`, so no
per-call sampling is required.

members.summary: Reduce bits per channel (Albumentations `Posterize`).
members.extended:
Two quantisation modes — pick based on whether you need
bit-exact Albumentations / cv2 parity or just a cheap float-only
posterise.

parameters.name: num_bits
parameters.description:
Number of bits to keep per channel; output has `2**num_bits`
distinct levels.  Must be in `[1, 7]`.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description:
Quantisation algorithm:

* `"uint8_mask"` — matches Albumentations / OpenCV
  bit-exactly.  Round-trips through `uint8` and applies the
  bit mask `~((1 << (8 - num_bits)) - 1)`, then divides by
  `255`.  Use this for parity with reference pipelines.
* `"float"` — pure-float quantisation via
  `floor(x * 2**num_bits) / 2**num_bits`.  Slightly
  different mid-bin placement than the OpenCV bit-mask;
  cheaper (no uint8 round-trip).  Use for novel pipelines
  where Albu parity isn't required.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

raises.description:
If `num_bits` falls outside `[1, 7]` or `mode` is not one
of the two recognised names.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.Posterize(num_bits=3, mode="uint8_mask", p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 32, 32))).data
>>> # 3-bit → 8 distinct quantisation levels per channel
>>> len(set(out.numpy().reshape(-1).tolist())) <= 8
True
notes:
The G3 parity suite verifies the `"uint8_mask"` mode matches
Albumentations to float32 epsilon across `num_bits ∈ {1, …, 7}`.
A 200-seed statistical aggregate (G4e) confirms the mean-of-max
diff stays in the same regime.

members.summary: Random gamma correction (Albumentations `RandomGamma`).
members.extended:
Samples a gamma value uniformly from `gamma_limit / 100` and
applies `img ** gamma` after clipping the input to `[0, 1]`.
Gamma `< 1` brightens midtones; gamma `> 1` darkens them.

(matches Albumentations' integer-percent convention).  Sampled

>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.RandomGamma(gamma_limit=(80, 120), p=1.0)

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `value` — the S-curve amount drawn uniformly
from `(-scale, scale)`; positive values brighten
midtones, negative values darken them.

members.summary: Apply a random S-shaped tone curve around midtones (Albumentations `RandomToneCurve`).
members.extended:
Samples an amount `a` uniformly from `(-scale, scale)` and adds
`a * sin(2 * pi * x)` to the clipped image.  The sine wave biases
midtones up (if `a > 0`) or down (if `a < 0`) while leaving the
deep shadows and bright highlights near identity, producing a
classic photographic S-curve.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Half-width of the symmetric range from which the tone-curve
amount is sampled (uniform `(-scale, scale)`).

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.RandomToneCurve(scale=0.1, p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 32, 32))).data
>>> tuple(out.shape)
(3, 32, 32)

members.summary: Sharpen an image via a 3x3 unsharp kernel blend (Albumentations `Sharpen`).
members.extended:
Convolves the image with a 3x3 Laplacian-of-Gaussian style kernel
whose centre weight is `8 + lightness`, then blends the sharpened
result with the original using a per-call `alpha`.  Higher
`alpha` puts more weight on the sharpened image; `lightness`
controls the centre-weight of the kernel and thus the strength of
the high-frequency boost.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Range from which the original / sharpened blend weight is
sampled uniformly per call.

parameters.name: lightness
parameters.description:
Range for the kernel-centre additive term `lightness`; larger
values give a stronger sharpen.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.Sharpen(alpha=(0.2, 0.5), lightness=(0.5, 1.0), p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 32, 32))).data
>>> tuple(out.shape)
(3, 32, 32)

members.summary: Invert pixel intensities above a threshold (Albumentations `Solarize`).
members.extended:
Samples a threshold uniformly from `threshold` (on the 0-255
scale, divided by 255 internally), then replaces every pixel
`x >= t` with `1 - x`, leaving pixels below the threshold
untouched.  Classic photographic posterisation effect, also used by
AutoAugment / RandAugment.

is interpreted as the constant range `(v, v)`; a tuple samples
the threshold uniformly per call.

>>> tf = T.Solarize(threshold=128, p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 32, 32))).data
>>> tuple(out.shape)
(3, 32, 32)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(blur_limit: tuple[int, int] = (3, 7), sigma_limit: float | tuple[float, float] = 0.0, alpha: tuple[float, float] = (0.2, 0.5), threshold: float = 10.0, p: float = 0.5)

methods.name: make_params

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `a` (residual blend weight from `alpha`),
`b` (Gaussian kernel side `k` stored as float so the
triplet stays homogeneous), and `c` (Gaussian sigma).

notes:
`k` is forced odd via `_odd`.  A non-positive `sigma`
falls back to OpenCV's kernel-derived
`0.3 * ((k - 1) * 0.5 - 1.0) + 0.8` heuristic.

members.summary: Unsharp-mask sharpen via `img + alpha*(img - blur)` (Albumentations `UnsharpMask`).
members.extended:
Subtracts a Gaussian-blurred copy from the original and adds the
high-frequency residual back, scaled by `alpha`.  This is the
photographic "unsharp mask" used to enhance edges without amplifying
noise as much as a raw Laplacian sharpen would.  The kernel size
`k` and Gaussian `sigma` are sampled per call from
`blur_limit` / `sigma_limit`; `sigma` defaults to the
OpenCV-style heuristic `0.3 * ((k - 1) * 0.5 - 1.0) + 0.8` when
the sampled value is non-positive.

parameters.name: blur_limit
parameters.description:
Inclusive range from which the Gaussian kernel size `k` is
sampled (snapped to the next odd integer).

parameters.name: sigma_limit
parameters.description:
Range from which the Gaussian standard deviation is sampled
uniformly.  A scalar `s` is treated as `(0, s)`; `0` falls
back to the kernel-derived heuristic above.

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description:
Range from which the residual blend weight is sampled
uniformly; higher values yield a stronger sharpen.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description:
Reserved for parity with the reference framework's
masked-residual variant; kept on the signature for forward
compatibility but currently unused (every pixel is sharpened).

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.UnsharpMask(blur_limit=(3, 7), alpha=(0.2, 0.5), p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 32, 32))).data
>>> tuple(out.shape)
(3, 32, 32)

members.summary: Mask random horizontal and vertical bands of the image (Albumentations `XYMasking`).
members.extended:
Samples `num_masks_x` vertical column-bands of width
`mask_x_length` and `num_masks_y` horizontal row-bands of height
`mask_y_length`, each placed at a uniformly-sampled offset.  All
sampled bands are unioned into a single multiplicative keep-mask
and applied via the standard mask + fill pattern.  Equivalent to

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `ops` — the resolved sequence of
`(op_name, signed_magnitude)` pairs after sampling one
sub-policy uniformly from the active policy table and
gating each sub-op by its per-policy probability.
Ops in `NO_MAGNITUDE_OPS` are emitted with
magnitude `0.0`; ops in `SIGNED_OPS` get a random
sign on the table-lookup magnitude.

members.summary: AutoAugment (Cubuk et al., 2019 — [arXiv:1805.09501](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.09501)).
members.extended:
Each call samples one of 25 fixed sub-policies uniformly; each
sub-policy is two `(op, probability, magnitude_idx)` triples
applied sequentially with their own probability gates.  Three
policy tables are shipped, verbatim from the paper:

* `"imagenet"` — Table 2 (default).  General-purpose vision
  classification.
* `"cifar10"` — Table 6.  Small-image classification.
* `"svhn"` — Table 7.  Digits / OCR-style data.

parameters.name: policy
parameters.description:
Which policy table to sample sub-policies from.  Must be one
of `"imagenet"`, `"cifar10"`, `"svhn"`.

parameters.name: num_magnitude_bins
parameters.description:
Magnitude bin count.  The paper / reference framework uses
`10` for AutoAugment (vs. `31` for RandAugment /
TrivialAugmentWide); the policy tables index into this size.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.utils.transforms import AutoAugment
>>> tf = AutoAugment(policy="imagenet")
>>> x = lucid.rand(3, 224, 224)
>>> y = tf(x)
>>> tuple(y.shape)
(3, 224, 224)
notes:
Photometric (image-only) by Lucid convention — see the module
docstring.

members.name: RandAugment
members.signature: RandAugment(num_ops: int = 2, magnitude: int = 9, num_magnitude_bins: int = 31, interpolation: str | Interpolation = Interpolation.BILINEAR, fill: float = 0.0, p: float = 1.0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_ops: int = 2, magnitude: int = 9, num_magnitude_bins: int = 31, interpolation: str | Interpolation = Interpolation.BILINEAR, fill: float = 0.0, p: float = 1.0)

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries `ops` — a length-`num_ops` tuple of
`(op_name, signed_magnitude)` pairs.  Op names are drawn
uniformly with replacement from the 14-op vocabulary; the
magnitude shared across all ops is fixed at the
`self.magnitude` lookup index, with a random sign for
ops in `SIGNED_OPS`.

members.summary: RandAugment (Cubuk et al., 2020 — [arXiv:1909.13719](https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.13719)).
members.extended:
Each call samples `num_ops` ops uniformly with replacement from
the 14-op vocabulary and applies them sequentially, all sharing
the same magnitude $M$ (signed per-op for symmetric ops).
Reduces AutoAugment's two-level search space (policy + magnitude)
to two scalar hyper-parameters that can be tuned with a simple
grid sweep.

parameters.name: num_ops
parameters.description:
Number of ops sampled per call (with replacement).  The
reference-framework default for ImageNet is 2.

parameters.name: magnitude
parameters.description:
Integer index into the magnitude lookup table; must satisfy
`0 <= magnitude < num_magnitude_bins`.  Reference-framework
recipes sweep this from 5 (small models) to 15 (large models).

parameters.name: num_magnitude_bins
parameters.description:
Magnitude bin count — same convention as
`TrivialAugmentWide`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.utils.transforms import RandAugment
>>> tf = RandAugment(num_ops=2, magnitude=9)
>>> x = lucid.rand(3, 224, 224)
>>> y = tf(x)
>>> tuple(y.shape)
(3, 224, 224)
notes:
Photometric (image-only) by Lucid convention — see the module
docstring.  The op draws are with replacement so a call may stack
the same op twice (e.g. `Rotate` followed by `Rotate`); this is
the reference-framework behaviour.

members.name: TrivialAugmentWide
members.signature: TrivialAugmentWide(num_magnitude_bins: int = 31, interpolation: str | Interpolation = Interpolation.BILINEAR, fill: float = 0.0, p: float = 1.0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_magnitude_bins: int = 31, interpolation: str | Interpolation = Interpolation.BILINEAR, fill: float = 0.0, p: float = 1.0)

parameters.name: img
parameters.description: Image tensor; not inspected, carried through for dispatch.

returns.description:
Carries the sampled `op_name` (uniform over the 14-op
vocabulary) and `magnitude` — either `0.0` for ops in
`NO_MAGNITUDE_OPS`, or
`magnitudes[mag_idx]` (with a random sign for ops in
`SIGNED_OPS`) where `mag_idx` is uniform over
`[0, num_magnitude_bins)`.

members.summary: Trivial Augment Wide (Müller & Hutter, 2021 — [arXiv:2103.10158](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.10158)).
members.extended:
Each call draws one op uniformly from the 14-op vocabulary and one
magnitude uniformly from `[0, num_magnitude_bins)`.  Despite its
simplicity it matches the accuracy of much more elaborate policies
(AutoAugment, RandAugment) on ImageNet — making it the default
"set-and-forget" augmentation in modern recipes.

parameters.name: num_magnitude_bins
parameters.description:
Magnitude bin count.  The wide variant uses 31; the original
TrivialAugment uses 10.  Larger values give a finer magnitude
grid.

parameters.name: interpolation
parameters.description:
Resample mode for the geometric ops (Shear / Translate /
Rotate).

parameters.name: fill
parameters.description: Border fill value for the geometric warps.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description:
Probability of applying the policy at all.  TrivialAugment is
designed to fire every call (Identity is one of the 14 ops, so
~7% of calls are no-ops by virtue of the op draw); the `p`
knob is provided for compositional consistency, not because
`p < 1` is recommended.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.utils.transforms import TrivialAugmentWide
>>> tf = TrivialAugmentWide()
>>> x = lucid.rand(3, 224, 224)
>>> y = tf(x)
>>> tuple(y.shape)
(3, 224, 224)
notes:
Photometric (image-only) by Lucid convention — see the module
docstring.

members.name: RandomErasing
members.signature: RandomErasing(p: float = 0.5, scale: tuple[float, float] = (0.02, 0.33), ratio: tuple[float, float] = (0.3, 3.3), value: float | tuple[float, ...] | str = 0.0)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(p: float = 0.5, scale: tuple[float, float] = (0.02, 0.33), ratio: tuple[float, float] = (0.3, 3.3), value: float | tuple[float, ...] | str = 0.0)

members.summary: Erase a random rectangular region of the image (Zhong et al., 2017).
members.extended:
At each call, with probability `p` one rectangle whose area is a
fraction of the image (sampled uniformly from `scale`) and whose
aspect ratio is sampled log-uniformly from `ratio` is replaced by
`value`.  If no rectangle fits after 10 attempts the call is a
no-op (matches the torchvision contract).

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the erase.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description:
Range of the erased-region area as a fraction of the image
area.  Sampled uniformly per call.

parameters.name: ratio
parameters.description:
Range of the erased-region aspect ratio (W / H).  Sampled
log-uniformly per call (matches the reference implementation).

parameters.name: value
parameters.annotation: float or tuple of float or {"random"}
parameters.description:
Fill value:

* `float` → fill the rectangle with this scalar (broadcast
  across channels).
* tuple of length `C` → per-channel constants (e.g. the
  ImageNet pixel mean).
* `"random"` → fill with i.i.d. samples from
  $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$, drawn fresh on each call.

examples:
Standard ImageNet recipe (p=0.25):
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.utils.transforms import RandomErasing
>>> tf = RandomErasing(p=0.25, scale=(0.02, 0.33), ratio=(0.3, 3.3))
>>> x = lucid.rand(3, 224, 224)
>>> y = tf(x)
>>> tuple(y.shape)
(3, 224, 224)
Per-channel ImageNet-mean fill:
>>> tf = RandomErasing(p=1.0, value=(0.485, 0.456, 0.406))
notes:
The erase is performed via a multiplicative keep-mask + additive
fill (same pattern as `CoarseDropout`) so the operation
composes cleanly with autograd and avoids any in-place writes.

The log-uniform aspect ratio means `ratio=(0.3, 3.3)` samples
landscape and portrait rectangles symmetrically, exactly matching
the spec in Zhong et al., 2017 §3.2.

members.summary: Standard ImageNet classification *training* preset (augmentation).
members.extended:
Pipeline (stages 4–7 conditional, all share the same Normalize stats):

1. RandomResizedCrop(crop_size, scale, ratio)
    2. HorizontalFlip(p=hflip_prob)            — skipped if hflip_prob = 0
    3. AutoAugment / RandAugment / TAW         — skipped if auto_augment = None
    4. ColorJitter(brightness=contrast=...)    — skipped if color_jitter = 0
    5. Normalize(mean, std, max_pixel_value=1)
    6. RandomErasing(p=random_erasing)         — skipped if random_erasing = 0

Mirrors the reference-framework training recipe shipped with most
classification models.  Setting `auto_augment` and/or
`random_erasing` reaches the strong-augmentation recipe used by
timm / torchvision references.

members.summary: Abstract base for task-specific preprocessing presets.
members.extended:
A preset is a thin wrapper over an internal `Compose`
pipeline (assembled in `__init__` from the preset's task-
specific defaults) plus a serialisation contract.  Subclasses
must:

1. Set the `preset_type` class variable to a unique short
   string used in `config.json`.
2. Declare the constructor's typed kwargs (no `**kwargs` —
   enforces the schema explicitly).
3. Build `self._pipeline` (a `Compose`) inside
   `__init__`.
4. Implement `_init_kwargs` returning the dict of constructor
   arguments needed to reconstruct an identical instance.

`preprocessor_type` key in `to_dict` / `from_dict`.

Samples `s` uniformly from `scale_limit` and resizes the image
to `(round(H * (1 + s)), round(W * (1 + s)))`.  Image uses the
requested `interpolation`, masks use nearest, boxes and
keypoints scale exactly.

methods.name: make_params

parameters.name: img
parameters.description:
Image tensor; its spatial size is read to compute the
expanded output canvas that fully contains the rotated
rectangle.

returns.description:
Carries the composed `(3, 3)` matrix (rotate about the
old centre, then translate into the centre of the new
canvas) and `out_hw` equal to the expanded
`(new_h, new_w)`.

notes:
`new_w = ceil(W*|cos| + H*|sin|)` and
`new_h = ceil(W*|sin| + H*|cos|)` so no rotated content is
clipped.

members.summary: Rotate by a random angle, expanding the canvas to keep all content (Albumentations `SafeRotate`).
members.extended:
Like `Rotate` but enlarges the output canvas so the full
rotated rectangle fits without clipping.  The new size is

$$
\; \lceil W\sin\theta + H\cos\theta\rceil\right),
$$

with $\theta$ sampled per call from `limit`.  The affine
matrix is built as "rotate about the old center, then translate
into the centre of the new canvas" — so straight rotations
(multiples of 90°) preserve pixel positions exactly within their
new canvas, and content never leaves the frame.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description:
Range from which the rotation angle (degrees, image-space CW)
is sampled uniformly.  A scalar `a` is treated as `(-a, a)`.

parameters.name: interpolation
parameters.description:
Resampling mode; accepts the integer / name aliases used by the
reference framework (`0=nearest`, `1=bilinear`).

parameters.name: border_mode
parameters.description:
Border handling mode (reference-framework convention).
`0` is constant fill with `value`; other modes mirror /
reflect the boundary.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Constant fill value used when `border_mode == 0`.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transform.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.SafeRotate(limit=(45, 45), p=1.0)
>>> out = tf(T.Image(lucid.rand(3, 24, 32))).data
>>> out.shape[0]               # channels preserved
3
>>> out.shape[-1] >= 32        # width expanded to fit rotated content
True

members.summary: Swap the H and W axes with probability `p` (Albumentations `Transpose`).
members.extended:
Exchanges the last two dimensions of the image and mask, and swaps
`(x, y)` coordinates for bounding boxes and keypoints so every
target sits in the new `(W, H)` canvas consistently.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Probability of applying the transpose.

extended:
An Albumentations-compatible transform library (matching class names +
constructor signatures) built tensor-native on Lucid (no numpy / PIL,
per H4).  Transforms consume / produce `lucid.Tensor` images
`(C, H, W)` or `(B, C, H, W)`; image *decoding* (file → tensor)
lives outside this package.

Two entry points:

* **Class transforms** — `Compose` a list of `Transform`
  objects.  Each is applied with probability `p` and moves every
  typed target (`Image` / `Mask` / `BoundingBoxes`
  / `Keypoints`) consistently.
* **Functional** — stateless ops under
  `lucid.utils.transforms.functional`.

Presets (`ImageClassification`) bundle a task's canonical
inference pipeline; pretrained weights ship preprocessing this way.

```python
>>> import lucid.utils.transforms as T
>>> tf = T.Compose([
...     T.SmallestMaxSize(256),
...     T.CenterCrop(224, 224),
...     T.Normalize(mean=(0.485, 0.456, 0.406), std=(0.229, 0.224, 0.225)),
... ])
>>> y = tf(image)
```
